name: Vishvanatha Cakravarti
core_thesis: Viśvanātha Cakravartī is the late-Gauḍīya consolidator who re-anchors *acintya-bhedābheda* in Vraja-experience after a generation of scholastic systematisation. He preserves the Six Gosvāmīs' settled doctrine — Kṛṣṇa as *svayaṃ-bhagavān*; the threefold *śakti*; the *jīva* as *taṭastha-śakti*; *bhakti* as *abhidheya* and *prema* as *prayojana* — and concentrates his constructive work on the inner morphology of *bhakti*: the eight stages from *śraddhā* through *prema* (*Mādhurya-Kādambinī*) and the technical structure of *rāgānugā-bhakti* (*Rāga-Vartma-Candrikā*), in which the *sādhaka* internally meditates on his *siddha-deha* in Vraja-līlā as the operative method for awakening *prema*. His Bhāgavata commentary, the *Sārārtha-Darśinī*, is the school's standard tenth-book gloss after Sanātana and Jīva. Across these works the metaphysical claim — that the *jīva*'s real distinctness and real non-difference from Kṛṣṇa hold simultaneously by *acintya-śakti* — is read inward, into the structure of the devotee's contemplative practice and experience.

engaged_works.title: Sararthadarshini on Bhagavata
engaged_works.summary: The *Sārārtha-Darśinī* is Viśvanātha's commentary on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* and, with Jīva's *Krama-Sandarbha* and Sanātana's tenth-book *Vaiṣṇava-Toṣaṇī*, one of the three principal Gauḍīya *Bhāgavata*-readings. It works through the entire purāṇa, but its centre of gravity is the tenth book, where Viśvanātha distils Sanātana, Jīva, and earlier Vaiṣṇava commentators (notably Śrīdhara Svāmin) into a settled Gauḍīya reading of Vraja-līlā. The commentary is doctrinally precise — Kṛṣṇa as *svayaṃ-bhagavān*, the *gopīs*' *prema* as the apex of *mādhurya-rasa*, the *nitya-siddha* status of Vraja-residents — and contemplatively oriented: many *līlā*-passages are glossed with explicit instructions for *smaraṇa* by the *rāgānugā* practitioner. As such it functions both as authoritative exegesis and as the standard Gauḍīya devotional reader of the *Bhāgavata*.

engaged_works.title: Madhurya-Kadambini
engaged_works.summary: The *Mādhurya-Kādambinī* ("cloud-bank of sweetness") is Viśvanātha's eight-chapter manual of the developmental stages of *bhakti*. Each *amṛta-vṛṣṭi* ("shower of nectar") treats one stage of the canonical Gauḍīya progression: *śraddhā*, *sādhu-saṅga*, *bhajana-kriyā*, *anartha-nivṛtti*, *niṣṭhā*, *ruci*, *āsakti*, *bhāva*, and finally *prema*. Drawing on Rūpa's *Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu* and Jīva's *Bhakti-Sandarbha*, Viśvanātha provides what neither systematic predecessor offers in this form: a phenomenologically precise account of how *bhakti* matures in the practitioner — the obstacles characteristic of each stage, the inner signs of progress, the points of regression, and the conditions for transition. The work is at once introspective psychology and metaphysical exposition, since the stages are read as the actualisation of the *jīva*'s eternal *taṭastha* constitution. It is the standard Gauḍīya manual of devotional progress.

engaged_works.title: Raga-Vartma-Chandrika
engaged_works.summary: The *Rāga-Vartma-Candrikā* ("moonlight on the path of rāga") is Viśvanātha's technical manual on *rāgānugā-bhakti* — the mode in which the *sādhaka* follows the spontaneous love (*rāga*) of the eternal *parikaras* (associates) of Kṛṣṇa in Vraja, particularly the *gopīs* in *mādhurya-rasa*. The text clarifies the relation between *vaidhī* and *rāgānugā* practice, the conditions of eligibility (*adhikāra*) for *rāgānugā* — including the central distinction between *bāhya-aṅga* (external practice in the practitioner's present body) and *antaraṅga* (internal *smaraṇa* in one's *siddha-deha*) — and the morphology of *bhāva* as it ripens through *līlā*-meditation. The work has a polemical edge: it is the school's response to misunderstandings of *rāgānugā* as licensing imitation of the *gopīs*' outward conduct and the canonical reference for the technical structure of internal contemplative practice. With the *Mādhurya-Kādambinī*, it forms the practical core of late-Gauḍīya devotional theology.
